entries.title: Smallest viable design
entries.summary: Add structure only for a demonstrated responsibility, variation, ownership, or quality-attribute pressure.
applicability: The request introduces a real boundary, variation, or measurable quality pressure.
preconditions: The concrete pressure and affected behavior can be named.
contraindications: The abstraction is justified only by a possible future need.
tradeoffs: A smaller design may require later evolution when new evidence appears.
questions: What is the smallest change that satisfies the observable scenario?
Which proposed abstraction has more than one credible reason to exist?

entries.title: Information hiding
entries.summary: Keep volatile implementation knowledge behind the responsibility that owns it.
applicability: A detail changes for reasons different from its callers.
preconditions: The owning responsibility and stable caller need can be identified.
contraindications: The proposed boundary merely mirrors file layout without hiding a decision.
tradeoffs: Encapsulation can add indirection and migration work.
questions: Which consumers truly need this knowledge?

entries.title: Dependency inversion
entries.summary: Keep stable policy independent of changing implementation details through an owner-aligned contract.
applicability: Stable policy currently depends directly on volatile infrastructure or integration details.
preconditions: A stable responsibility-facing contract can be named.
contraindications: There is one stable implementation and no boundary or testing pressure.
questions: What must remain stable when the implementation changes?

entries.title: Single state owner
entries.summary: Give mutable state one clear owner or make synchronization and invalidation explicit.
applicability: The change adds or redistributes mutable state.
preconditions: Readers, writers, lifetime, and invalidation events can be identified.
contraindications: The value is immutable or entirely local to one operation.
tradeoffs: Central ownership may create coordination or throughput pressure.
questions: Who creates, mutates, invalidates, and observes the state?

entries.title: Cache-aside reference
entries.summary: A caller or repository checks a cache before loading the source and explicitly owns population and invalidation.
applicability: Repeated reads have measurable cost and bounded staleness is acceptable.
preconditions: Stable keys, invalidation policy, and source fallback are defined.
contraindications: Strong consistency is required without a coherent invalidation mechanism.
tradeoffs: Improves repeated-read latency but introduces stale data, miss concurrency, and memory pressure.

entries.title: Adapter reference
entries.summary: Translate an external or legacy contract at a boundary owned by the consuming system.
applicability: An external contract differs from the stable internal responsibility model.
preconditions: Translation, error mapping, and version ownership are identifiable.
contraindications: The external contract already is the intentional internal contract.
tradeoffs: Adds translation code and can hide useful provider capabilities.
questions: Which provider concepts must not leak?

entries.title: Strategy reference
entries.summary: Represent independently varying behavior behind one caller-owned contract.
applicability: At least two credible behaviors vary independently of the caller.
preconditions: Selection owner, lifetime, fallback, and stable inputs/outputs are known.
contraindications: There is only one fixed behavior or variants do not share a coherent contract.
tradeoffs: Improves variation isolation but adds selection and lifecycle complexity.
questions: Can behavior change during a task?

entries.title: Observer reference
entries.summary: Notify independent consumers through an explicit subscription and delivery lifecycle.
applicability: A publisher should not depend on independent consumer behavior or results.
preconditions: Ordering, subscription lifetime, delivery, and failure semantics are defined.
contraindications: The interaction requires a synchronous result or strict transactional coupling.
tradeoffs: Reduces direct coupling but increases temporal reasoning and observability needs.
questions: How are failures and duplicate delivery handled?
